questions.q: Identify the single security control whose absence allowed the DarkSide attackers to access Colonial Pipeline's network.
options: Multi-factor authentication on the VPN account
An intrusion detection system on the corporate network
End-to-end encryption of the pipeline's operational data
A firewall between the IT and operational technology networks
questions.explanation: The breach turns entirely on this point: the VPN account was protected by a password and nothing else, so a single leaked credential was the whole of the defence. Multi-factor authentication adds a second, independent proof — something you have or are — which would have made the stolen password useless on its own. A firewall or IDS might have helped later, but the missing gate at the front door was MFA.

questions.q: Outline how the DarkSide attackers initially obtained valid login details for Colonial Pipeline's VPN.
options: They sent phishing emails to employees and tricked one into revealing their password
They exploited an unpatched software vulnerability in the VPN server
They used a password from a batch of leaked credentials, most likely reused from another breached site, for a dormant VPN account
They physically broke into an office and read a password written on a sticky note
questions.explanation: The script states the password turned up among a batch of leaked credentials — most likely the employee had reused it somewhere else that had been breached, so it was floating around for criminals to find. The account was no longer in active use but still worked. Phishing (A) and unpatched software (B) are common attack vectors but are not what the investigation found here.

questions.q: Explain why a password alone is described in the episode as a fragile defence.
options: Passwords are slow to type, so users choose short ones that are easy to crack
Passwords expire too quickly, so accounts are often left without any protection
A password is only 'something you know', and it can be stolen, guessed, leaked, or reused — once an attacker has it, they effectively become you
Modern computers can brute-force any password within a few seconds regardless of length
questions.explanation: The episode frames a password as a single factor — 'something you know' — that can be stolen, guessed, leaked, or reused, and once compromised the attacker is indistinguishable from the real user. That is why critical access should never depend on a single factor. Brute-forcing (D) is a real risk but is not the mechanism the case turns on; the Colonial password was leaked, not cracked.

questions.q: Distinguish between the two factors a multi-factor login combines, as described in the episode.
options: Something you know (a password) and something you have or are (a phone code or fingerprint)
A username and a matching email address held by the same person
A primary password and a backup password stored in a different system
A public key and a private key generated by the same certificate
questions.explanation: Multi-factor authentication pairs a first factor — 'something you know', the password — with a second, independent proof: 'something you have', like a code on a phone or a hardware token, or 'something you are', like a fingerprint. The strength comes from the factors being independent, so stealing the password does not give the attacker the phone. Two passwords (C) are both 'something you know' and so are not genuinely independent factors.

questions.q: Describe why Colonial Pipeline shut down the entire physical pipeline even though the ransomware hit only its IT and business systems.
options: The ransomware directly seized the industrial controls that move fuel through the pipes
They could not be certain the attackers had not reached operational systems, and with billing systems frozen they could not account for or bill the fuel they would deliver
Government regulators legally ordered an immediate full shutdown
The decryption tool required the pipeline to be offline before it could run
questions.explanation: The investigation found the ransomware hit the IT/business side — billing and the corporate network — not the operational controls directly. Colonial shut down anyway for two reasons the script names: they could not be fully certain the attackers had not reached the operational systems, and with billing frozen they could not account for and bill the fuel. A purely digital attack on the back office halted the physical flow of fuel.

questions.q: Explain the accountability lesson the episode draws from the dormant VPN account.
options: Employees should be required to change their passwords every thirty days
Accounts should never be given access to more than one system at a time
All passwords should be stored in a central encrypted vault for auditing
Every account that lingers past its usefulness is an unguarded door, so organisations must know which accounts exist, who owns them, whether they are still needed, and disable the ones that are not
questions.explanation: The account was reportedly dormant yet still live and able to log in — a valid door into the network that nobody was watching. The accountability discipline is knowing exactly which accounts exist, who they belong to, whether they are still needed, and disabling the ones that are not, so the attack surface does not quietly grow. Frequent password rotation is a different control and would not have closed an unused account.

questions.q: Which security principle does the episode argue was the real casualty of the attack, and why does it matter so much here?
options: Confidentiality, because the stolen 100 GB of data was the most damaging part of the incident
Integrity, because the attackers altered the records of who owed money for fuel
Authentication, because the password was the only thing that was compromised
Availability, because the loss of a vital, running service — the fuel supply for half a coast — became a matter of public order and national security
questions.explanation: The episode is explicit that the data was never the real story — the cars lining up for petrol were. What the attack destroyed was availability: the ability of a vital service to keep running and delivering. When the unavailable thing is the fuel supply for half a country, availability stops being a technical nicety and becomes a matter of public order and national security, which is why protecting critical infrastructure is treated so seriously.

questions.q: Compare how Colonial actually recovered its systems with the ransom payment it made.
options: The decryption tool restored everything quickly, so the backups were never needed
Colonial paid about $4.4M in Bitcoin and received a decryptor, but it was so slow that they restored much of their systems from their own backups anyway
Colonial refused to pay and relied entirely on the FBI to unlock the encrypted files
The backups had also been encrypted, so paying the ransom was the only way to recover
questions.explanation: Colonial paid roughly $4.4 million in Bitcoin and received a decryption tool, but it was famously so slow that much of the real recovery work was done from their own backups. This shows that tested backups, not the ransom, did the heavy lifting — a core business-continuity lesson. The FBI later clawed back much of the Bitcoin, but that happened after the damage, and the agency did not unlock the files (C).

questions.q: A hospital runs a remote-access VPN for staff and stores patient records on its internal network. Applying the Colonial Pipeline lesson, which control most directly addresses the root cause of that breach?
options: Encrypt all patient records at rest so stolen data is unreadable
Increase internet bandwidth so the VPN can handle more simultaneous users
Require multi-factor authentication on every VPN account so a leaked or reused password is not enough to log in
Buy cyber-insurance so the cost of any future ransom is covered
questions.explanation: The root cause at Colonial was single-factor remote access: one leaked password let the attackers walk in because there was no second gate. Requiring MFA on every VPN account directly closes that hole, making a stolen or reused password a dead end. Encrypting records protects confidentiality but would not have stopped a valid login, and buying cyber-insurance manages cost after the fact rather than preventing the intrusion.

questions.q: A council's water-treatment provider asks you to recommend the priorities of an incident response plan, using the framework from this episode. Which set of priorities best matches the lessons drawn from Colonial Pipeline?
options: Detect the intrusion, pay any ransom immediately to restore service, then notify the public
Detect the intrusion, contain it by isolating affected systems, restore from tested offline backups, then review and harden by enabling MFA and removing stale accounts
Restore from backups first, then detect how the attackers got in, and finally disable the firewall to speed recovery
Notify regulators, wait for the attackers to provide a decryptor, and rebuild only the operational systems
questions.explanation: The episode's response lessons are detect, contain, restore from tested backups, then review and harden — the organisations that weather an attack plan this in advance rather than improvising. Containment (shutting down/isolating) limits the damage, and tested offline backups did Colonial's real recovery, while enabling MFA and removing stale accounts hardens against a repeat. Paying the ransom first (A) is exactly the controversial step Colonial regretted, since the decryptor was too slow to rely on.
